topic: I bought an engraved engagement ring at an estate sale and a photograph in the estate shows me proposing with it eleven years in the future
title: I Bought an Engagement Ring at an Estate Sale. The Inscription Names a Woman I Have Not Met Yet. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I bought a gold engagement ring at an estate sale in Tulsa last October for forty dollars. The inscription inside the band reads FOR DANIELLE OCTOBER 2030. I am thirty nine years old. I have never met a woman named Danielle. A jeweler in Tulsa traced the work order. The ring was custom commissioned in two thousand twenty four. The man who commissioned it was a retired watchmaker named Augustus Reed. He had no children. He had no fiancee. He had Alzheimer's.
description:
I bought a gold engagement ring at an estate sale in Tulsa last October for forty dollars. The inscription inside the band reads FOR DANIELLE OCTOBER 2030. I am thirty nine years old. I have never met a woman named Danielle. A jeweler in Tulsa traced the work order. The ring was custom commissioned in two thousand twenty four.

The estate sale was on Birch Street in a small bungalow that had belonged to a retired watchmaker named Augustus Reed. He had been a master jeweler for fifty years at a downtown shop called Reed and Sons. He had no children. He was unmarried. The woman running the sale was a court appointed estate manager. Augustus had been declared incapable two years before his death due to advancing Alzheimer's. He had still been finishing custom commissions until the day his license was revoked.

There is a sealed envelope I found in his study that I have only opened once. The envelope contained a single photograph and a handwritten note. The photograph shows me at age forty seven proposing to a woman in a public park. The note describes the day in detail. Watch to the end. The ring is currently in a safety deposit box at a bank I do not bank with. I cannot remember opening the account.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I bought a gold engagement ring at an estate sale in Tulsa last October for forty dollars. The diamond was a half carat. The setting was a simple six prong solitaire. The inside of the band was engraved in fine cursive. The inscription read FOR DANIELLE OCTOBER 2030. I am thirty nine years old. I have never met a woman named Danielle. I bought the ring because the seller said it would be melted down for scrap if no one took it. I put it in my coat pocket and forgot about it until I got home. That night I read the inscription under a desk lamp with a magnifying glass.

sections.narration: The estate sale was on Birch Street. A small white bungalow with a brick chimney and a maple tree in the front yard. The house belonged to a man named Augustus Reed who had died the previous spring. The woman running the sale was a court appointed estate manager named Lillian Goss. She told me Augustus had been a master jeweler for fifty years at a downtown shop called Reed and Sons. He had no children. He had been unmarried his entire life. He had lived alone in the bungalow since nineteen seventy four. She charged me forty dollars in cash for the ring and gave me a printed receipt with his name on it.

sections.narration: I have been going to estate sales since two thousand seventeen. About one a weekend. I buy jewelry mostly. Old engagement rings, watch movements, brooches, anything with a hallmark. I clean them up and sell the pieces I do not want on a small website I run. I have a spreadsheet of every piece I have bought, what I paid, and what I sold it for. The Augustus Reed ring was the most unusual hallmark I had seen. The maker's mark inside the band was a small letter A inside a circle. I knew that mark. It was Augustus's own mark. The ring had been made by the man whose estate was selling it.
image_prompts: wide interior of a small home jewelry workshop with neatly organized tools and a magnifying lamp over a workbench, dim warm light, no people, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a laptop screen displaying a spreadsheet listing jewelry pieces with columns for date purchased and price, dim desk lamp light, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of a hand writing a new entry in a spreadsheet column on a laptop screen, only the hand visible, dim ambient light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The next morning I drove to Reed and Sons. The shop had been bought by a new owner named Henry Kline who had kept the old name. Henry had worked under Augustus for twelve years and was happy to talk about him. I showed him the ring. He pulled out a leather bound work order book from a drawer and turned to the page for the commission. The entry was in Augustus's handwriting. Dated September two thousand twenty four. The client name listed for the commission was blank. The line was empty. The inscription instructions were written in full. FOR DANIELLE OCTOBER 2030. The deposit had been paid in cash. The final pickup was listed as pending.
image_prompts: wide interior of a small downtown jewelry shop with glass display cases and a wood paneled counter, dim warm light, no people visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

close detail of a gold engagement ring resting on a black velvet pad on a glass jewelry counter, dim overhead spotlight, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: Henry told me Augustus had been diagnosed with Alzheimer's in two thousand twenty two. The disease progressed slowly at first. By two thousand twenty four his hands still worked but his memory was fragmenting. He had been declared legally incapable in early two thousand twenty five and his license to commission custom work was revoked. He continued small repairs at home until his death. Henry assumed the Danielle ring had been a phantom commission. An order Augustus had imagined and entered into the ledger out of habit. Augustus had a habit of taking deposits in cash and keeping no records of who handed him the bills. I almost left it there. I drove home and put the ring in a drawer.

sections.narration: Three weeks later Henry called me. He had been going through Augustus's other custom work orders from his final years. Twenty seven rings commissioned between two thousand twenty two and two thousand twenty four. All with inscriptions. All with client name lines left blank. Every inscription named a woman and a month and a year. The earliest year inscribed was two thousand twenty nine. The latest was two thousand sixty one. Twenty seven rings. Twenty seven future dates. Henry had located six of the rings already through pawn shops and estate sales around Tulsa. He sent me photographs of the inscriptions. The handwriting on every inscription was identical to mine.
image_prompts: close detail of a laptop screen displaying multiple photographs of ring inscriptions in different gold bands, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I returned to the bungalow on Birch Street. Lillian had not yet finished clearing the house. She let me into Augustus's home study. The room was small and lined with bookshelves. The workbench held watchmaking tools laid out neatly as if for a project the next day. In the center of the workbench was a small leather portfolio. Inside the portfolio were twenty seven sealed envelopes. Each envelope was addressed in Augustus's handwriting to a name I did not recognize. One envelope was addressed to me. My full first name. Middle initial. Last name. The envelope had been sealed with red wax stamped with the small letter A inside a circle.
image_prompts: wide interior of a small home study lined with bookshelves and a wooden workbench in the center, dim afternoon light, no people, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a leather portfolio open on a workbench with twenty seven cream colored envelopes neatly stacked inside, dim lamp light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a single sealed envelope with a red wax seal stamped with a small letter A inside a circle, dim ambient light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a cream envelope addressed in fountain pen with a full first name middle initial and last name visible, dim lamp light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a wooden workbench with watchmaking tools laid out in a neat row beside a leather portfolio, dim home study light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I took my envelope home. I left the other twenty six in the portfolio. Lillian agreed to keep them sealed until each recipient could be found. I have helped her locate eleven of the recipients in the months since. They are all real people. Each one bought a ring from Augustus's estate, the same way I did. Each one received their sealed envelope. Each one has refused to tell me what their envelope contained. Lillian has begun searching for the remaining recipients. Two have already proven harder to locate. The names match no living person in any public record. Lillian believes those envelopes are addressed to people who have not been born yet.

close detail of a hand writing a list of recipient names on a yellow legal pad beside an open envelope, only the hand visible, dim lamp light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I opened my envelope last December. Inside was a single photograph and a handwritten note. The photograph was a color print on heavy stock. It shows a man kneeling on one knee in a public park in autumn. He is holding out the gold ring I bought from Augustus's estate. A woman is standing in front of him with her hand over her mouth. The man in the photograph is me. He is older. His hair has greyed at the temples. The note in the envelope was written in Augustus's hand. It described the day. The park. The name of the woman. The time. The date written at the bottom of the note was October eleventh two thousand thirty.
image_prompts: close detail of a hand opening a wax sealed envelope on a wooden desk under a lamp, only the hand visible, dim home office light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I put the ring in a safety deposit box at a bank in downtown Tulsa. The bank is one I have never used. When I went to open the account the manager told me the box was already in my name. The account had been opened in person on a Tuesday in March of two thousand twenty four. The signature on file was mine. I do not remember being in Tulsa that month. I have not been able to confirm whether I traveled there. The box held nothing when I opened it. The ring is in there now. I drive past the bank some weekends. The lights are always on inside even on Sundays. The bank does not open on Sundays.
